metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.

records.culinary_uses: Some Gyroporus chestnut boletes are edible, but this guide treats the borealis/smithii record conservatively until the species split is better confirmed regionally.
records.notes: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.

mushroom_profile.summary: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.
research_notes: Species boundary remains conservative; do not promote as food without stronger regional species-level confidence.
Gyroporus records should be checked for brittle/hollow stem tissue, dry cap, lack of strong reticulation, and association with hardwoods or mixed woods.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.mushroom_card_note: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.
records.field_identification: Gyroporus records should be checked for brittle/hollow stem tissue, dry cap, lack of strong reticulation, and association with hardwoods or mixed woods.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food mushroom in this guide; keep as a cautious/ID record unless a local expert confirms the collection.
records.notes: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.

mushroom_profile.summary: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Check for a dusky cap, brownish pore surface, dark spore-print group, and flesh/stem staining tendencies. It is easy to confuse with other dark Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-like boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.
records.mushroom_card_note: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.
records.field_identification: Check for a dusky cap, brownish pore surface, dark spore-print group, and flesh/stem staining tendencies. It is easy to confuse with other dark Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-like boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Do not collect as food. Its importance here is as a caution/look-alike for king boletes.
records.notes: False King Bolete: a dangerous king-bolete look-alike concern in the Great Lakes region; do not treat as porcini.

mushroom_profile.summary: False King Bolete: a dangerous king-bolete look-alike concern in the Great Lakes region; do not treat as porcini.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, Great Lakes/eastern North America.
research_notes: No verified public-usable image is currently attached; record is suppressed from normal cards until an exact image is verified.
Compare any “king bolete” carefully against Boletus huronensis: duller cap, yellowish pores at maturity, blue staining in some tissues, and Great Lakes reports matter.

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat porcini-like boletes with wrong pores, wrong staining, hemlock association, or uncertain ID.
records.mushroom_card_note: False King Bolete: a dangerous king-bolete look-alike concern in the Great Lakes region; do not treat as porcini.
records.field_identification: Compare any “king bolete” carefully against Boletus huronensis: duller cap, yellowish pores at maturity, blue staining in some tissues, and Great Lakes reports matter.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: King-bolete look-alike associated with severe gastrointestinal illness; useful for recognizing unsafe porcini-like mushrooms.

records.suppression_reason: No verified public-usable Boletus huronensis image; placeholder art removed. Keep as high-risk caution/search record until a real image is verified.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus huronensis
use_links.notes: Supports False King Bolete / Boletus huronensis caution context for porcini-like boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime edible in this guide; mostly a recognizable hardwood bolete for comparison.
records.notes: Gray Bolete: drab gray oak/hardwood bolete with a coarsely reticulate stem that yellows from the base upward.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gray Bolete: drab gray oak/hardwood bolete with a coarsely reticulate stem that yellows from the base upward.
research_notes: Image rights metadata still needs direct verification before clearing all review/image debt.
Gray cap, whitish-to-gray pore surface, coarse stem reticulation, and yellowing lower stem are the useful checks.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.culinary_uses: Mild Tylopilus species are sometimes eaten, but this one is not a beginner target and must be separated from bitter Tylopilus species.
records.notes: Indecisus Bolete: mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores that bruise brown and a mostly whitish, non-olive stem.

mushroom_profile.summary: Indecisus Bolete: mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores that bruise brown and a mostly whitish, non-olive stem.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Key checks: pinkish mature pore surface bruising brown, mild taste, soft flesh, fairly equal whitish stem, and hardwood/oak association.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target in this guide; included for completeness and comparison.
records.notes: Leather-colored Bolete: brown/yellow-pored hardwood bolete; keep as a regional comparison rather than a prime edible.

mushroom_profile.summary: Leather-colored Bolete: brown/yellow-pored hardwood bolete; keep as a regional comparison rather than a prime edible.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Use brown leather-colored cap, yellowish pore surface, hardwood context, and lack of strong dangerous-color signals to compare against porcini-like boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition; keep separate from bitter Tylopilus and other brown boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Lilac Bolete.
staining: Flesh not staining; pores may bruise deeper yellow or brownish
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.
pore_color: White young; yellow to brownish yellow mature

records.edibility_detail: Do not use as a generic brown bolete; confirm the full field-trait set before eating.
records.field_identification: Oak/hardwood bolete with young lilac-brown to liver-red tones, yellow mature pore surface lacking olive shades, stem often flushed with liver color, reticulation possible, and white flesh that does not stain.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus separans
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, yellow pores, non-staining flesh, and liver-red stem flush.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus separans
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, lilac/liver-toned field traits, current Boletus placement, and edible-use as Choice.

records.edibility_notes: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition; keep separate from bitter Tylopilus and other brown boletes.

edible_use.notes: Cook confirmed young, firm specimens; do not use as a generic brown bolete.

images_structured.title: Lilac-brown bolete / Xanthoconium separans field view

records.quality_notes: High-value porcini ally with edible-use source support when oak/hardwood habitat, yellow-not-olive mature pores, liver-red/lilac stem flush, mild taste, and non-staining flesh all fit.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed young, firm specimens; do not use as a generic brown bolete.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusing a choice porcini ally with other brown boletes, bitter boletes, or false-king types.
records.hard_rule: Do not use as a generic brown bolete; confirm the full field-trait set before eating.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a food target here; useful as a distinctive dark bolete comparison.
records.notes: Lilac-brown Bolete: dark purplish-brown bolete with scaber-like stem dots and brown young pores; not a casual edible.

mushroom_profile.summary: Lilac-brown Bolete: dark purplish-brown bolete with scaber-like stem dots and brown young pores; not a casual edible.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Look for purplish-brown cap, brown young pore surface, and dark Leccinum-like scabers on the stem.

records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.notes: Noble Bolete: porcini-like hardwood bolete; useful, but keep regional taxonomy and look-alikes in view.

mushroom_profile.summary: Noble Bolete: porcini-like hardwood bolete; useful, but keep regional taxonomy and look-alikes in view.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Check for king-bolete style stature, pore color, stem reticulation, lack of strong blue bruising, and hardwood association.
v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus nobilis
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, oak/beech porcini-like field traits, taxonomy note, and edible-use as Good.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target; taste may be mild or bitter, and the value is mostly identification interest.
records.notes: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Strong yellow reticulation over the stem, yellow pore surface, yellow flesh, and little to no blue bruising are the big field marks.

records.mushroom_card_note: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.
records.field_identification: Strong yellow reticulation over the stem, yellow pore surface, yellow flesh, and little to no blue bruising are the big field marks.
records.edibility_detail: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
records.risk_reason: Taste can be bitter or unreliable; keep it as a recognition record unless a specimen is confidently identified and mild.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
